serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Assam Prawn in Singapore is approximately 150g, equivalent to 4–6 medium-to-large prawns coated in tamarind chilli sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Assam Prawn — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Assam Prawn (cr_id F03060001). All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this canonical Singapore food composition source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate for low-carbohydrate prawn/seafood dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Assam Prawn. Carbohydrate content is very low at 2.72g/100g, predominantly from tamarind and chilli. GI is estimated at approximately 15 (low) by analogy with other low-carbohydrate seafood and sauce-based dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 150g serving = (15 × 2.72 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.61, rounded to 0.6. This is clinically negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 723mg per 100g — exceeds the 600mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Assam Prawn due to its very high sodium content (723mg/100g), driven by belacan (shrimp paste), added salt, and natural prawn sodium; if consumed, request reduced belacan and no added salt, and restrict portion to no more than 80–100g per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.72g/100g) and high protein (16g/100g) support stable postprandial blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Assam Prawn is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes given its very low carbohydrate content (2.72g/100g), minimal sugar (2.17g/100g), and high protein (16g/100g); pair with low-GI staples rather than white rice and verify that the sambal sauce contains no excessive added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (723mg/100g) is a cardiovascular risk factor; fat profile is relatively lean with low saturated fat (~0.9g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (723mg/100g) in Assam Prawn poses a cardiovascular concern and should be moderated; however, the low saturated fat (~0.9g/100g), absence of trans fat, and lean protein from prawns are favourable, so small portions with reduced-salt preparation are advisable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chilli (nightshade) and high sodium may aggravate inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Prawns provide lean protein and some omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis; however, chilli is a nightshade ingredient that may aggravate joint symptoms in nightshade-sensitive individuals, and the high sodium (723mg/100g) may worsen RA-related tissue swelling — request reduced belacan and salt when ordering.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Assam Prawn (亚参虾; Udang Asam) is a classic Singaporean and Peranakan seafood dish consisting of whole or shelled prawns fried and simmered in a thick, tangy, and spicy sauce made from tamarind (assam), dried chilli, belacan (fermented shrimp paste), onion, garlic, and a small amount of sugar. The dish is characterised by its bold sour-spicy flavour profile, with the tamarind providing a distinctive tartness that balances the heat of the chilli and the umami depth of the belacan. The prawns are typically cooked shell-on to retain moisture and flavour, resulting in a tender, juicy texture coated in a glossy, intensely flavoured sauce. Nutritionally, Assam Prawn is high in protein (16g/100g) and low in carbohydrates (2.72g/100g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Fat content is moderate at 3.88g/100g with low saturated fat (~0.9g/100g). The primary clinical concern is its very high sodium content (723mg/100g), driven by belacan, added salt, and the natural sodium of prawns, which makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or those on sodium-restricted diets without modification. Calcium (86mg/100g) and iron (0.59mg/100g) contribute modestly to micronutrient intake. The glycemic index is estimated as low (~15) due to negligible carbohydrate content, making it appropriate for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when paired with low-GI staples. Assam Prawn is a staple of Singaporean hawker culture and home cooking, commonly served as a main dish alongside steamed rice or as part of a shared meal.

Is Assam Prawn suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03060001), which is the canonical primary source for Singaporean food composition. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches. Confidence is set to high as the exact dish is present in the approved primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Assam Prawn is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish as of 2026. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for Assam Prawn and are not available from an approved secondary source for this specific dish preparation as of 2026. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as trans fat is absent in this dish based on its ingredients (no partially hydrogenated oils). Saturated fat estimated at approximately 0.9g/100g based on prawn lipid composition and cooking oil contribution, consistent with HPB data context. GI is an analogous estimate (approximately 15, low) as no direct GI measurement for Assam Prawn exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; estimation is based on the negligible carbohydrate content (2.72g/100g) of the dish. FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology codes are approximate matches as no exact entry for Assam Prawn exists in these ontologies as of 2024.
